title: Mineralocorticoid receptor deletion alters spontaneous behavior in an automated home-cage monitoring apparatus

screening.reason: The study reports behavioral outcomes in mice using an automated home-cage monitoring apparatus, which is relevant to the review question.
matched_inclusion_criteria: Reports behavioral, social, preference, dominance, activity, or interaction outcomes

overall_concept_summary: This study investigates how mineralocorticoid receptor deletion affects spontaneous behavior in mice using automated home-cage monitoring.
background_rationale: The paper aims to understand the impact of genetic modifications on mouse behavior through automated monitoring, which can provide insights into social and activity behaviors.
methods_summary: Mice were monitored in an automated home-cage system for spontaneous behavior over a period of one month. The study used a mineralocorticoid receptor knockout model compared to wild-type controls.
results_summary: Mineralocorticoid receptor deletion led to significant changes in social interaction and activity levels, as measured by the automated monitoring apparatus.
conclusion_summary: The study concludes that genetic modifications can significantly alter spontaneous behavior patterns in mice, highlighting the utility of automated home-cage monitoring for behavioral research.
relevance_to_review: Highly relevant as it uses an automated home-cage system to monitor social and activity behaviors in mice.
key_takeaways: Mineralocorticoid receptor deletion alters spontaneous behavior in mice
Automated home-cage monitoring is a useful tool for studying mouse behavior
objective: To investigate the effects of mineralocorticoid receptor deletion on spontaneous behavior in mice using automated home-cage monitoring.
study_design: Experimental study with genetic modification and behavioral observation through an automated system.
population_or_sample: Mineralocorticoid receptor knockout mice and wild-type controls
methods: Genetic modification of mice to create a mineralocorticoid receptor knockout model
Monitoring in an automated home-cage system for one month
main_results: Mineralocorticoid receptor deletion significantly altered social interaction and activity patterns compared to wild-type controls.
conclusions: Genetic modifications can impact spontaneous behavior in mice, as demonstrated by changes observed through automated home-cage monitoring.
Automated systems provide valuable data for behavioral studies.
limitations: The study focuses on a specific genetic modification and may not be generalizable to other models or conditions.
